cards.text: The subscript operator {{c1::[]}} must be implemented as a {{c2::member function}}, not a friend or normal function.
operator[] should return by {{c1::reference}} to allow both reading and modifying: array[5] {{c2::=}} 10.

cards.question: What does this subscript operator do?
cards.answer: Allows accessing IntList elements using array syntax: list[3]. Returns reference to allow both reading and writing.
cards.explanation: Returns int& so you can do: list[3] = 5; or int x = list[3];

cards.front: Why do you need both const and non-const versions of operator[]?
cards.back: The non-const version (returns T&) allows modification. The const version (returns const T&) is called on const objects and prevents modification. This allows operator[] to work correctly with both const and non-const objects.

cards.question: What's the const version of this operator[]?
cards.answer: const int& operator[](int index) const { return m_data[index]; }
cards.explanation: Const version has const qualifier and returns const reference, preventing modification.

cards.text:
For const-correctness, provide two versions of operator[]:
{{c1::T&}} operator[](int) for non-const objects
{{c2::const T&}} operator[](int) {{c3::const}} for const objects

cards.front: Can operator[] take types other than int as the index parameter?
cards.back: Yes. The parameter can be any type: string for maps, custom types, etc. For example: map["key"] uses std::string as index. The parameter type depends on your use case.

cards.text: operator[] typically does {{c1::not}} check bounds. For safety, provide a {{c2::at()}} function that throws an exception on invalid index.

cards.front: Why must operator[] be a member function?
cards.back: The C++ standard requires operator[], operator(), operator->, and operator= to be member functions. This ensures they're called on an actual object and prevents ambiguity in overload resolution.

cards.explanation:
Standard pattern for subscript operator:
  • Must be a member function
  • Return by reference (T&) to allow modification
  • Provide const version (const T& operator[] const)
  • Parameter type matches your indexing needs (int, string, etc.)
  • Usually no bounds checking (use at() for safety)
examples: const T& operator[](int i) const { return m_data[i]; }

cards.front: What happens if you don't provide a const version of operator[]?
cards.back: Const objects won't be able to use operator[]. The compiler will give an error because calling a non-const member function on a const object is not allowed. Always provide both versions for full functionality.

cards.text: std::vector<T>::operator[] does {{c1::not}} perform bounds checking, while std::vector<T>::{{c2::at}}() throws std::{{c3::out_of_range}} if the index is invalid.
